If we want to influence ethically, then, it's very important that we do our homework in advance of our decision to influence others, to make sure that what we claim is true, naturally present, and not contrived or exaggerated in any way. And also to make sure that if our request is accepted, that the interests of our partner will be elevated in the process, along with our own interests. This is the case because the research on the long-term consequences of unethical influence is clear. Credibility craters. No doubt, each of us can recall a time when we were tricked or swindled in an economic exchange. None of us wants to be someone that others consider untrustworthy or even just risky to work with. The consequences are disastrous. There's yet another reason to avoid unethical influence attempts. They also damage the self-concepts of those who employ them regularly. They tell their users, so it looks like I'm a cheat who can't rely on my own strengths and capacities to succeed. I have to cheat to win.
